text:
I have been relatively quiet this week in the midst of such volatility and with very good reason because I am on a break in China (my very maiden trip here).

What I have seen and experienced so far related to energy:

1️⃣ High speed train reaching a speed of 348 km/h from Beijing to Tianjin, electric powered, no less

2️⃣ Busy roads and highways, quieter than most because of influx of EV vehicles. But it doesn't mean no traditional transport fuelled cars though.

3️⃣ Lots of English words everywhere but not much spoken (good luck with ordering food in a random shop!)

4️⃣ Amazing skyscrapers and construction in many places. Tianjin in particular surprised me with vibrant lights and laser show on the facade of multiple buildings 

5️⃣ A strong sense of culture steeped in thousands of years of history yet striving for innovation and hunger for success

Can't say I have witnessed demand destruction first hand.

Photo taken at Temple of Heaven where I heard some Americans talking to a local about Elon Musk being there recently. I was thinking of Trump more as I walked up those steps!

U.S. Petroleum Depletion Imposed Deadline on Hormuz War

Since the start of the war with Iran, depletion of the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) has bought time for the U.S. government to develop both diplomatic and military options to re-open the Strait of Hormuz. 

The United States has explored diplomatic negotiations via Pakistan and other mediators, as well as attempts to unblock the Strait by force, organising via virtual convoys using aircraft and drones to protect tankers in the waterway. 

Some tankers carrying crude, liquefied natural gas (LNG) and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) have successfully transited as a result of diplomatic agreements or under the armed protection of the United States.  

Since announcing a ceasefire in early April, the United States has pursued a mixed strategy of bombing-while-negotiating, holding talks while trying to increase the number of transits under cover of darkness. 

The intention appears to have been to pressure Iran into accepting a deal while at the same time degrading Iran’s ability to threaten shipping in case the talks fail. 

But with petroleum inventories at home running down rapidly, top U.S. policymakers have faced an increasingly urgent deadline to decide whether to reach an agreement with Iran or attempt to re-open the Strait fully by force. 

Ultimately, the White House seems to have decided the embarrassment of a diplomatic deal achieving less than its stated war aims was a lesser evil than the potential risks from trying to force the Strait open against hostile fire ...

The cost of shipping #containers continues to surge, with the Drewry Composite Index rising 12% over the past week, extending its seven-week gain to nearly 80%. Freight rates on the key Shanghai–New York and Shanghai–Rotterdam routes have almost doubled during that period, driven by a "perfect storm" of overlapping geopolitical, regulatory, and seasonal supply chain pressures.

In essence, a surge that isn't driven by a boom in global consumer spending, but rather an artificial rush of buying for vessel space to outrun tariffs, regulatory deadlines, and extended transit times at a time of elevated fuel costs. 

The index has risen 79% YTD and 21% over the past twelve months.

Maritime traffic via the Strait of Hormuz will only return to normal levels when mines are removed and traditional routes restored, some of the world's largest shipping organizations said June 18 while calling for more clarity on transit rules and coordination among authorities to allow safe passage. https://t.co/xouak9yKnr

The Fed's dot plot this week came in more hawkish than expected, and it's still driving everything. The 10-year yield is at 4.479%, the dollar held firm overnight, and the higher-for-longer path is now in the official projections, not just the whisper network.

Gold hit $4,121 this morning, touching the 4H demand zone at $4,107-$4,131 and bouncing back to $4,164. Five consecutive daily lower closes. PDL at $4,201 is now resistance, not support.

The macro and the chart are telling the same story. Iran's geopolitical risk premium faded as a broader deal framework took shape. Technically, the bounce from $4,121 is valid, but PDL hasn't been reclaimed, there's no CHoCH on the 1H, and the 4H keeps printing lower highs.

Don't buy this bounce. Juneteenth makes today's liquidity thin and moves unreliable. With the macro actively hawkish, the risk-reward of a countertrend long is poor.

PDL $4,201.35 is the line. A close above it with volume changes the picture and forces a reassessment. A rejection or stall there keeps the short case intact. A break below $4,121 opens $4,085, then $4,046.

Short targets: $4,095 and $4,046. Countertrend long from DZ1 ($4,107-$4,131) exits at $4,164 and $4,201. No holding past PDL.

If the Iran talks fall apart rather than just pause, the geopolitical bid returns fast. WTI crude reversing above $68 is the early tell.

Macro and chart agree gold is heavy. Let the market show you otherwise before trading the other way.

Physical oil is flowing and inflation fears are cooling

The initial agreement brokered by President Donald Trump achieved three critical successes for the Strait of Hormuz: the immediate de-escalation of military conflict, the removal of the U.S. naval blockade, and the immediate unhindered flow of commercial and Saudi-flagged oil tankers through the vital international waterway. [1, 2, 3]

1. Immediate De-escalation and Reopening
The memorandum of understanding achieved the immediate termination of military operations across all fronts and secured a commitment to reopen the Strait of Hormuz toll-free. This ended the maritime standoff that had severely crippled energy supply chains and global trade flows.

2. Lifting of the U.S. Naval Blockade
As a core term of the diplomatic framework, the United States agreed to lift its naval blockade on Iranian ports. This allowed Iranian forces to stand down and paved the way for international maritime traffic to safely transit the waterway without fear of direct military interdiction.

3. Unhindered Transit for Commercial Shipping
Hours after the signing of the initial accord, ship-tracking data confirmed that major energy shipments, including millions of barrels on Saudi-flagged supertankers, resumed routine and unhindered transits through the strait. This marked the restoration of normal energy transport after weeks of vessels going dark or actively avoiding the region. US crude inventories are at their lowest since 1985, yet WTI timespreads are not signalling the tightness you might expect.   

In the week ending 12 June, US crude stocks drew by 8.3 mb, taking outright levels to their lowest since 1985. Cushing fell to levels near operational minimums, and PADD 2 stocks hit their lowest since 2014. But the latest 40 mb SPR exchange, higher Permian crude inflows to Cushing and the announcement of a US–Iran MoU are all working to ease the near-term supply picture.   

Across products, the MoU is already shifting trade economics: jet export demand from the Gulf Coast is expected to slow, while Asian LPG buyers have adopted a wait-and-see stance ahead of potential freight rate easing through Hormuz. Our latest US Oil Weekly breaks down the draw across all the barrels.   

Read more: https://t.co/byGRebrgDK

#US #CrudeOil #Inventories 🛢️⛽️

📍US commercial crude oil inventories are reaching critical levels:

Crude inventories at Cushing, Oklahoma, the largest commercial storage hub in the US and the pricing point for WTI Crude, dropped -1.6M 🛢️ last week, to 20M 🛢️, (..) https://t.co/MY2ylY7YUP

Cushing, Oklahoma crude inventories have fallen to their lowest level since October 2014 according to the latest data.

Eight consecutive weekly draws have pushed stocks just above the roughly 20 million barrel level widely viewed as the hub’s functional “tank bottom.” Cushing is a key U.S. oil storage hub and serves as the physical settlement point for West Texas Intermediate futures.

While total capacity is near 90 million barrels, headline storage figures may overstate usable supply, as a portion of remaining crude can include sludge or material that is difficult to move operationally.

The drawdown is part of a broader decline in U.S. crude inventories. The Department of Energy report showed a national crude drawdown of 8.26 million barrels, compared with a consensus estimate of 3.5 million barrels.

Source: The Deep Dive

FACTS AND SOURCES AVOUT GAS PRICES  : 
Biden announced the 180 million barrel SPR release in March 2022.

February 24, 2022 — Russia invaded Ukraine.
March 31, 2022 — President Biden announced the release of 180 million barrels from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), at a rate of about 1 million barrels per day for six months.
April–October 2022 — The oil was released.
October 19, 2022 — The administration announced the sale of the final 15 million barrels from that program.
November 8, 2022 — U.S. midterm elections were held.
The SPR inventory fell from roughly 568 million barrels in early 2022 to about 372 million barrels by late 2022, the lowest level in decades.

SOURCES 

U.S. Treasury (July 2022): Strategic Petroleum Reserve release announced March 2022.
U.S. Department of Energy (Nov. 2024): References the 180 million barrel emergency sale in 2022.
Axios (Oct. 19, 2022): “Biden taps oil reserve in attempt to curb gas prices.”
Al Jazeera (Oct. 19, 2022): “Biden releasing 15 million barrels of oil from strategic reserve.”
Ohio Capital Journal (Oct. 20, 2022): Release completed the 180 million barrel plan and would hit markets after the election.  

Yes. The 180 million barrel Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) release was announced by President Biden on March 31, 2022, with the plan to release 1 million barrels per day for 180 days in response to rising oil prices following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.  

The part that fuels the political debate is what happened right before the 2022 midterm elections:

On October 19, 2022, less than three weeks before the November 8 midterms, Biden announced an additional 15 million barrel release, completing the previously announced 180 million barrel program.  
Multiple news reports noted the timing coincided with a campaign season where high gas prices and inflation were major political issues for Democrats.  
The administration’s stated reason was to lower fuel prices and offset market disruptions from the Ukraine war and OPEC+ production cuts.  

So the timeline is:

March 31, 2022 – Announced 180 million barrel release.
April–October 2022 – Oil released over six months.
October 19, 2022 – Final 15 million barrels announced just before the midterm election.
November 8, 2022 – Midterm election.

FACTS AND SOURCES : 
Biden announced the 180 million barrel SPR release in March 2022.

February 24, 2022 — Russia invaded Ukraine.
March 31, 2022 — President Biden announced the release of 180 million barrels from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), at a rate of about 1 million barrels per day for six months.
April–October 2022 — The oil was released.
October 19, 2022 — The administration announced the sale of the final 15 million barrels from that program.
November 8, 2022 — U.S. midterm elections were held.
The SPR inventory fell from roughly 568 million barrels in early 2022 to about 372 million barrels by late 2022, the lowest level in decades.

SOURCES 

U.S. Treasury (July 2022): Strategic Petroleum Reserve release announced March 2022.
U.S. Department of Energy (Nov. 2024): References the 180 million barrel emergency sale in 2022.
Axios (Oct. 19, 2022): “Biden taps oil reserve in attempt to curb gas prices.”
Al Jazeera (Oct. 19, 2022): “Biden releasing 15 million barrels of oil from strategic reserve.”
Ohio Capital Journal (Oct. 20, 2022): Release completed the 180 million barrel plan and would hit markets after the election.
